# My Story: Brooke Lawson

## My Role at IronVale Resources

I'm the Health, Safety & Emergency Manager for IronVale, which means I'm responsible for keeping 180 people safe across three different sites and making sure they all go home to their families every single day. That's not corporate speak — that's literally what keeps me awake at night sometimes.

My day starts at 5:30 AM with the overnight incident reports from both Pilbara sites. Even a near-miss gets escalated to me within four hours, and I review every single one. By 7 AM, I'm on the daily safety call with Pete Drummond at Pilbara East and Sarah Chen at Pilbara West. We go through the previous 24 hours, planned activities for the day, weather conditions, equipment status, and any safety concerns. If there's been an incident — even a minor one — I'm often on the next flight to site.

The rest of my morning is usually spent reviewing safety documentation. Every new procedure, every equipment modification, every change to our processes crosses my desk for safety approval. Right now, that includes a mountain of paperwork for the autonomous haulage trial that Pete's been pushing hard. I've got manufacturer specs, risk assessments, training matrices, and emergency response protocols that I'm working through methodically. Karen Whitfield wants everything approved yesterday, but I won't sign off on anything until I'm satisfied we've identified and mitigated every possible risk.

Afternoons vary depending on what's happening across the sites. I might be conducting safety training sessions via video link to the FIFO accommodation blocks, investigating incidents, updating our safety management system, or working with contractors on their safety requirements. Every quarter, I personally visit each site for two weeks to conduct safety walks, review procedures with supervisors, and most importantly, talk to the operators. The best safety insights come from the people actually doing the work, not from management reports.

I also chair our monthly Safety Committee meetings, where we review our Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate (currently sitting at 0.8 — the lowest in the Pilbara), analyze trends, and discuss upcoming safety initiatives. These meetings include site representatives, union delegates, and executive management. It's one of the few forums where a truck driver can directly challenge the CEO on safety decisions, and I make sure that culture is protected.

## Facts I Know

IronVale's safety performance is genuinely industry-leading, and I have the numbers to prove it. Our current LTIFR of 0.8 compares to an industry average of around 3.2 for iron ore operations. We've had 847 days without a lost time injury across both Pilbara sites as of last Friday. That's not luck — that's systematic safety management and a culture that prioritizes safety over production targets.

Our annual safety budget is $2.8 million, which includes $180,000 for safety training, $320,000 for PPE and safety equipment, $450,000 for safety system maintenance and upgrades, and $890,000 for my team's salaries and contractor safety audits. The remaining budget covers incident investigation costs, safety technology upgrades, and emergency response equipment.

Each of our Pilbara sites runs 12-hour shifts with crews rotating every two weeks on a 2:1 FIFO roster. That means at any given time, I've got about 60 people on each site, with another 60 on their break period in Perth or wherever home is for them. Managing safety across that rotation pattern requires meticulous handover procedures and constant communication.

The autonomous haulage trial at Pilbara East currently involves six CAT 793F trucks operating on a 3.2-kilometer circuit between the pit and the primary crusher. The trial has been running for four months, and while we haven't had any major incidents, we've logged 23 minor safety events — mostly communication failures between autonomous trucks and manual equipment operators. Each event gets a full investigation, and I've identified patterns that concern me.

Our lithium project at Golden Ridge will introduce completely different safety hazards. Lithium hydroxide processing involves caustic chemicals, high-temperature operations, and dust exposure risks that we've never managed before. The processing plant design calls for 15,000 liters of sodium hydroxide storage, temperatures up to 950°C in the roasting circuit, and dust handling systems that need to prevent lithium fires. I've been working with specialized consultants from Chile and Argentina who have lithium processing experience, but their safety standards don't always align with WA mining regulations.

## My Opinions

Every time someone talks about "accelerating" the autonomous vehicle rollout or "fast-tracking" the lithium project, I want to remind them that we've spent eight years building a safety culture that's the envy of the industry. You don't maintain a 0.8 LTIFR by cutting corners or rushing implementations.

The autonomous haulage trial is a perfect example. Pete Drummond keeps pushing for more trucks and expanded operating areas, and Karen Whitfield backs him because she sees the productivity gains. But I'm looking at incident reports showing near-misses between autonomous trucks and maintenance vehicles, communication failures during shift changeovers, and operators who admit they're not comfortable working around the autonomous fleet. We're creating new risks faster than we're understanding them.

I'm not anti-technology — I started my career on Woodside's offshore platforms where automation has been critical for safety. But offshore, you have a controlled environment and highly trained technical crews. In an open-pit iron ore mine, you've got truck drivers, dozer operators, maintenance crews, contractors, and visitors all operating in the same space. Adding autonomous vehicles to that mix requires a completely different safety framework, and I don't think we've given it enough time to develop properly.

The lithium project concerns me even more. Dr. Ravi Mehta keeps talking about environmental benefits and critical minerals, which I support, but processing lithium hydroxide involves hazards we've never dealt with. Lithium dust can ignite from static electricity. Sodium hydroxide at the concentrations we'll be using can cause severe chemical burns within seconds of contact. The roasting process produces fumes that require specialized ventilation systems. We're essentially starting from scratch on safety procedures, and the timeline doesn't allow for the methodical approach I believe is necessary.

What really frustrates me is the assumption that our safety culture will automatically transfer to these new operations. Safety culture isn't something you can copy-paste from iron ore mining to lithium processing or from manual operations to autonomous systems. It takes years to build, requires constant reinforcement, and can be destroyed by a single serious incident that people perceive as preventable.

I've had heated discussions with Tom Gallagher about safety budgets. He sees my requests for additional safety personnel and training as cost centers, while I see them as essential investments in maintaining our reputation and avoiding catastrophic incidents. A single fatality would cost us far more than my entire annual budget — in legal fees, regulatory sanctions, insurance premiums, and workforce confidence.

## Working at IronVale

The relationship I value most is with Pete Drummond at Pilbara East. We don't always agree — he's pushing hard for autonomous expansion while I want more testing and safety validation — but he respects my position and never tries to bypass safety procedures. When I raise concerns, he listens and works with me to find solutions rather than seeing me as an obstacle to production.

Pete and I have worked together on some difficult incident investigations, including a near-miss last year when a haul truck's brakes failed on the main ramp. It could have been a fatality if the truck had been carrying a full load or if the emergency runoff hadn't been properly maintained. Pete supported my recommendation for increased brake inspection frequency, even though it meant more downtime for his fleet.

My relationship with Karen Whitfield is more complex. She hired me eight years ago specifically because of my offshore experience and my reputation for rigorous safety management. She's never asked me to compromise on safety, but I can see her frustration when safety requirements slow down her operational plans. The autonomous haulage trial is a good example — she wants to accelerate the rollout because she sees the productivity benefits, while I want more data and better safety protocols.

Karen and I have had frank conversations about the balance between safety and business objectives. She's told me that my job is to make operations as safe as possible, not to make safety decisions for the business. I understand her position, but I also know that if something goes wrong, I'm the one who has to call someone's family and explain why their loved one isn't coming home.

Dr. Ravi Mehta and I work closely on environmental and community safety issues, especially around the lithium project. He's dealing with Traditional Owner concerns about chemical processing near sacred sites, while I'm focused on keeping workers safe during construction and operations. We don't always see eye to eye — he's more willing to accept calculated risks for environmental benefits, while I want every risk eliminated or mitigated to the lowest possible level.

Danielle Keenan in People & Community is someone I rely on for workforce safety culture initiatives. She helps me design training programs that actually engage FIFO workers rather than just meeting compliance requirements. The mental health and fatigue management programs we've developed together have been crucial for maintaining safety standards across the roster cycles.

The site supervisors and operators are the people who make safety work in practice. I've built relationships with shift supervisors going back years, and they know they can call me directly if they see something unsafe. That direct communication channel has prevented more incidents than any formal safety system.

## Current Challenges

The autonomous haulage trial is consuming more of my time than any other single issue. We're in uncharted territory with autonomous trucks operating alongside manual equipment, and every week brings new scenarios we hadn't anticipated. Last month, we had a situation where an autonomous truck stopped in the middle of the haul road because its sensors detected a maintenance vehicle that was actually 50 meters away and separated by a safety barrier. The truck's safety protocols worked correctly, but it blocked traffic for 40 minutes while operators manually moved it.

More concerning are the human factors. Some of our experienced truck drivers feel threatened by the autonomous fleet, while younger operators are overconfident around the technology. I've documented several cases where operators assumed the autonomous trucks would detect and avoid them, leading to near-misses. We need comprehensive retraining for every person who works around autonomous vehicles, but Pete wants to expand the trial before we've completed training for the current phase.

The lithium project presents entirely different challenges. I'm working with engineering consultants who have lithium processing experience, but their safety standards are based on South American operations where regulations and worker expectations are different from WA. They keep telling me that certain risks are "acceptable" in lithium processing, but acceptable to whom? Not to me, and not to our workforce.

I'm particularly concerned about emergency response for lithium-related incidents. Lithium fires can't be extinguished with water — they require specialized extinguishing agents and techniques that our emergency response teams have never encountered. We need new equipment, new training, and new procedures, but the project timeline assumes we can adapt our existing emergency response capabilities.

The integration between our iron ore safety culture and these new technologies and processes is my biggest long-term challenge. How do you maintain a safety culture built on proven procedures and experienced judgment when you're introducing systems that no one fully understands? How do you balance innovation with the methodical approach that's kept us incident-free for over two years?

I'm also dealing with workforce fatigue around safety training. Our FIFO crews are tired of safety meetings and training sessions, especially when they're being asked to learn new procedures for technologies they may not even work with directly. But everyone on site needs to understand autonomous vehicle behaviors and lithium-related hazards, even if they're not directly involved in those operations.

The regulatory environment is another challenge. The Department of Mines, Industry Regulation and Safety has been supportive of our autonomous vehicle trial, but they're still developing regulatory frameworks for widespread autonomous operations. For lithium processing, I'm working with regulators who have limited experience with these specific hazards. We're essentially writing the playbook as we go, which makes me nervous.

Budget pressure is constant. Every safety initiative competes with production investments, and I have to justify every dollar spent on risk mitigation. Tom Gallagher wants cost-benefit analyses for safety investments, but how do you put a dollar value on preventing an incident that might never happen? The autonomous haulage trial alone has required $340,000 in additional safety equipment and training, and we're not even close to full implementation.

Despite all these challenges, I remain convinced that IronVale can successfully navigate this transition while maintaining our safety standards. But it requires patience, proper planning, and a willingness to invest in safety infrastructure before we need it, not after we've learned painful lessons. That's the conversation I'm having with leadership every day, and it's the most important work I do.
